results.possibleIdentity: Michael Danon (born c. 1937–1938) is an engineer and industrialist associated with Automatic Steam Products Corporation and Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Born in Zagreb, Croatia, to a Sephardic Jewish family, he became a refugee during the Nazi occupation, eventually settling in Long Beach, New York. He graduated from the Stevens Institute of Technology in 1960 with a background in mechanical engineering. Professionally, Danon was a key figure at Automatic Steam Products Corporation (later known as Sussman-Automatic Corp.), located at 3 West 61st Street in New York City. The company specialized in the manufacturing of steam generators, industrial boilers, and water demineralizers, such as the 'Midget Aqualab.' In the late 1960s and early 1970s, Danon was active in technical fields spanning mechanical engineering and architecture, and he was part of the collaborative movement between the arts and sciences facilitated by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In his later years, he has been recognized as a donor to his alma mater, Stevens Institute of Technology, and has been involved with the Jasenovac Research Institute in New York.
